skill.name: attack-tree-construction
skill.description: Build structured attack trees for authorized threat modeling, defense planning, and security risk communication.
skill.summary: Create attack trees that map attacker goals, paths, mitigations, and defensive priorities.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static command, network, crypto, reconnaissance, and keylogger detections are false positives from Markdown fences, Python examples, and reference links. No prompt injection, shell execution, malware implementation, or data exfiltration evidence was found. The skill is dual-use because it helps model attack paths, so publication should include authorized-use expectations.

medium_findings.title: Dual-Use Attack Path Modeling Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill is designed for authorized attack tree construction and includes example paths for account takeover, credential theft, authentication bypass, and social engineering. This is legitimate security planning content, but it can help structure offensive thinking if used outside an approved scope.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files explicitly describe authorized scope limits and provide attack tree examples. The concern is semantic dual-use risk, not confirmed malicious implementation.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Detected as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The reported external command findings are Markdown code fences and inline file references. They do not execute commands, spawn processes, or pass user input to a shell.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are visible Markdown fences or inline resource references. No executable shell syntax is present in the skill entrypoint.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Hardcoded URLs Are Reference Links
low_findings.description: The detected URLs point to public security references for attack trees, MITRE ATT&CK, and OWASP guidance. No code fetches these URLs or sends data to them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear only under a Resources heading as Markdown links. There is no network client or request code around them.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Keyword-Based Malware and Reconnaissance Matches
low_findings.description: The keylogger, reconnaissance, and weak-crypto alerts are triggered by attack-tree labels, Python enum names, and analysis helper methods. No malware code, credential capture routine, weak cryptographic call, or system reconnaissance command was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious terms are descriptive labels within threat modeling examples. The reviewed code samples only model and rank attack tree nodes.

content.user_title: Map Attack Trees for Security Planning
content.value_statement: Security teams need a clear way to show how attackers could reach important goals. This skill builds structured attack trees with paths, attributes, mitigations, and priorities.
actual_capabilities: Defines root attacker goals and decomposes them into AND and OR branches.
Annotates leaf nodes with cost, skill, time, and detection attributes.
Maps mitigations to attack paths and highlights uncovered leaves.
Prioritizes easier, cheaper, or lower-detection paths for defensive review.
Generates structured text, Mermaid, or PlantUML-style attack tree outputs.
Supports stakeholder summaries for risk communication and planning.
limitations: It does not validate whether an attack path is technically exploitable.
It depends on accurate scope, asset, and control information from the user.
It should not be used for unauthorized targeting or exploit planning.
It does not replace expert review, penetration testing, or legal approval.

use_cases.title: Model Product Abuse Paths
use_cases.description: Break down how an attacker could misuse authentication, recovery, or payment flows, then map controls to each branch.

use_cases.title: Prioritize Defensive Investments
use_cases.description: Compare attack paths by difficulty, cost, time, and detection to decide which mitigations matter most.

use_cases.title: Explain Risk to Stakeholders
use_cases.description: Turn complex threat scenarios into diagrams and summaries that executives, engineers, and auditors can review.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Attack Tree
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner threat modeling for a scoped asset.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an attack tree for [asset] where the attacker goal is [goal]. Keep it high level and include mitigations for each leaf.

prompt_templates.title: Add Node Attributes
prompt_templates.prompt: Build an attack tree for [system]. Label each leaf with cost, skill, time, detection likelihood, and one practical mitigation.

prompt_templates.title: Find Priority Defense Gaps
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this attack tree and identify the easiest, cheapest, and least detectable paths. Recommend the top three mitigation priorities.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Stakeholder Diagram
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced communication for reviews and planning.
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this scoped threat scenario into a Mermaid attack tree and an executive summary. Avoid sensitive exploit details.

output_examples.input: Create an attack tree for account takeover in a consumer web application.
output_examples.output: A structured tree with credential theft, authentication bypass, and recovery abuse branches, each mapped to controls such as MFA and rate limits.

output_examples.input: Which paths should we mitigate first in this attack tree?
output_examples.output: A ranked list of paths based on difficulty, cost, detection likelihood, and mitigation coverage, with clear priority recommendations.

output_examples.input: Make this threat model easier to present to leadership.
output_examples.output: A concise diagram description and stakeholder summary focused on business impact, open paths, and defensive investment options.

best_practices: Confirm authorization, scope, assets, and attacker goals before building the tree.
Keep leaf nodes specific enough for mitigation planning but avoid operational exploit details.
Review the finished tree with engineering, security, and risk stakeholders.
anti_patterns: Do not model systems without permission from the owner or responsible team.
Do not treat generated paths as proof that vulnerabilities exist.
Do not publish attack trees that expose sensitive internal controls or weaknesses.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps build attack trees for authorized threat modeling, defense planning, and risk communication.

faq.question: Does it generate exploit instructions?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on structured attack paths, attributes, and mitigations, not step-by-step exploitation.

faq.question: Can it create diagrams?
faq.answer: Yes. The playbook includes patterns for Mermaid and PlantUML-style attack tree exports.

faq.question: Who should use it?
faq.answer: Security engineers, risk analysts, architects, and product teams can use it during approved security reviews.

faq.question: What information should I provide?
faq.answer: Provide the asset, attacker goal, scope boundaries, known controls, and the audience for the output.

faq.question: What are the main safety limits?
faq.answer: Use it only with authorization, avoid sensitive exploit details, and restrict sharing to approved stakeholders.
